title: Asiakaspalvelu
description: Practice Finnish phrases for phone calls, hotel check-in, and restaurants
instructions: Learn the most useful customer service phrases for real-life situations in Finland.

questions.text: How do you introduce yourself at the start of a Finnish phone call?
questions.explanation: 'Täs on [nimi]' is the natural spoken Finnish way to start a call — literally 'This is [name].' It uses puhekieli (spoken language): täs = tässä.

questions.text: How do you say 'I'm calling to ask...' in Finnish?
questions.explanation: 'Soitan kysyäkseni...' = I'm calling to ask... This is a formal/polite construction using the translative infinitive.

questions.text: How do you say 'I would like to book an appointment' on the phone?
options: All of the above are correct
questions.explanation: All are correct! 'Haluaisin varata ajan' (I would like to book), 'Mun täytyy varata aika' (I need to book), 'Voinko varata ajan?' (Can I book?). They're just different levels of formality.

questions.text: You want to try Finnish on the phone. What do you say?
options: Mä opiskelen suomea ja haluaisin yrittää käydä keskustelun suomeksi.
questions.explanation: 'Mä opiskelen suomea ja haluaisin yrittää käydä keskustelun suomeksi.' = I'm studying Finnish and I'd like to try having the conversation in Finnish. A great phrase to practice with native speakers!

questions.text: How do you ask to switch to English on the phone?
questions.explanation: 'Voinko puhua englanniksi?' = Can I speak in English? This is polite and clear. You could also say 'Voisimmeko puhua englanniksi?' (more formal).

questions.text: How do you say 'Hello, I have a reservation for two nights' at a hotel?
options: Hei, mulla on varaus kahdeksi yöksi.
Hei, mulla on varaus kahdessa yössä.
Hei, mulla on varaus kahden yön.
Hei, mulla on varaus kahdelle yölle.
questions.explanation: 'Hei, mulla on varaus kahdeksi yöksi.' = Hello, I have a reservation for two nights. 'Kahdeksi yöksi' uses the translative case to express purpose/duration.

questions.text: How do you say 'I have a reservation under the name Salo'?
questions.explanation: 'Mulla on varaus nimellä Salo.' — 'Nimellä' uses the adessive case (-lla) to mean 'under the name'. This is a fixed expression.

questions.text: The receptionist says 'Saisinko henkilöllisyystodistuksen?' What are they asking for?
questions.explanation: 'Saisinko henkilöllisyystodistuksen?' = May I see your ID? 'Henkilöllisyystodistus' = identity document/ID. 'Saisinko' is a polite conditional form of 'saada' (to get/receive).

questions.text: The receptionist says 'Katsotaas...' What does this mean?
questions.explanation: 'Katsotaas' = Let me check / Let's see. It comes from 'katsoa' (to look/check) + -taas (a softening particle). Very common in service situations.

questions.text: The receptionist says 'Kirjoittaisitko henkilötiedot tähän lomakkeeseen?' What do they want you to do?
options: Fill in your personal details on this form
questions.explanation: 'Kirjoittaisitko henkilötiedot tähän lomakkeeseen?' = Could you fill in your personal details on this form? 'Kirjoittaisitko' is a polite conditional question.

questions.text: How do you ask 'Do you have a table for two?' at a restaurant?
questions.explanation: 'Hei, olisiko teillä tilaa kahdelle?' = Hello, do you have a table for two? 'Olisiko' is a polite conditional form. 'Tilaa' = room/space (partitive). This is the most natural phrasing.

questions.text: How do you say 'There are four of us, is there a table for us?'
options: Meitä on neljä, löytyisikö meille pöytää?
questions.explanation: 'Meitä on neljä, löytyisikö meille pöytää?' = There are four of us, is there a table for us? 'Meitä' = us (partitive), 'löytyisikö' = would there be found (polite conditional of löytyä).

questions.text: The staff says 'Nyt just on täyttä, mutta tuolta vapautuu kohta yksi pöytä.' What does this mean?
options: It's fully booked right now, but a table will be free soon
We have a table for you now
questions.explanation: 'Nyt just on täyttä' = It's full right now. 'Tuolta vapautuu kohta yksi pöytä' = a table will be free soon. 'Vapautua' = to become free/available. Positive news!

questions.text: The staff says 'Joo, valitkaa vaan mieleinen pöytä.' What should you do?
questions.explanation: 'Joo, valitkaa vaan mieleinen pöytä.' = Yes, just choose any table you like. 'Valitkaa' is the polite imperative of 'valita' (to choose). 'Mieleinen' = one you like.

questions.text: The staff asks 'Jäättekö odottamaan?' What are they asking?
options: Do you want to order now?
questions.explanation: 'Jäättekö odottamaan?' = Will you wait? 'Jäädä' = to stay/remain, 'odottamaan' = to wait (illative form of the 3rd infinitive). They're asking if you'll wait for a table.

questions.text: What is the correct order for starting a Finnish phone call?
options: State purpose → Say your name → Try Finnish
Say your name first → State why you're calling → Try Finnish first
Ask a question → Say your name → Wait
Greet → Say the company name → Ask
questions.explanation: In Finnish phone calls: 1) Say your name first ('Täs on [nimi]'), 2) State why you're calling ('Soitan kysyäkseni...'), 3) Try Finnish first if you're studying it.

questions.text: How do you say 'Hello and welcome!' in Finnish? (Said by receptionist)
questions.explanation: 'Hei ja tervetuloa!' is the standard welcome greeting. 'Tervetuloa' = welcome (literally 'came well').

questions.text: How do you say 'I don't have a reservation, but do you have a table for one?'
options: Mulla ei oo varausta, mut olisko teillä tilaa yhdelle?
questions.explanation: 'Mulla ei oo varausta, mut olisko teillä tilaa yhdelle?' This is perfect spoken Finnish: 'ei oo' instead of 'ei ole', 'mut' instead of 'mutta'. Natural puhekieli!

questions.text: The receptionist says 'Joo, täältä löytyy varaus Salo-nimellä.' What does this mean?
options: Sorry, there's no reservation for Salo
Yes, we have a reservation under the name Salo
Yes, Salo is your room number
questions.explanation: 'Täältä löytyy varaus Salo-nimellä' = We have (lit. 'from here is found') a reservation under the name Salo. 'Löytyy' = is found/exists. Good news!

questions.text: Which phrase is most polite for booking an appointment on the phone?
questions.explanation: 'Haluaisin varata ajan' is the most polite — 'haluaisin' is conditional ('I would like'). 'Mun täytyy varata aika' is more urgent ('I need to'). 'Mä tahdon' and 'anna mulle' are too direct.
